ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic curry rice dish entry; no specific Sukiya Deluxe Curry Rice entry exists. Mapped to closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers curry rice as a composite dish; no sub-code exists for the deluxe variant with karaage and egg toppings.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Deluxe Curry Rice is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous portion of steamed white rice topped with Japanese beef curry sauce, two pieces of chicken karaage, and one half-boiled egg.

nutrient_source.title: Deluxe Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for Deluxe Curry Rice (cr_id G00010221). Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite analogy (beef curry with rice, ~1.2 g/100 g); see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and curry rice composite analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Sukiya Deluxe Curry Rice. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese-style curry rice (white rice base with curry sauce), which reports GI values in the range 60–70. A midpoint of 65 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 25.19 × 4.5) / 100 ≈ 29.3 g per 450 g serving (net carbohydrate used: 25.19 g/100 g × 4.5 = 113.4 g; GL = 65 × 113.4 / 100 = 73.7 — note: GL reported here is per 100 g serving equivalent scaled to 450 g portion: 65 × 25.19 / 100 × 4.5 = 7.37 × 4.5 ≈ 29.3).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1040 mg sodium, representing 45% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-sodium curry roux and soy-based seasoning on the karaage to keep single-meal sodium below 600 mg; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K vs 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets vascular risk but does not eliminate it at full-serving quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with estimated GI 65 (medium); a 450 g serving delivers approximately 113 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~29.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion to 150–200 g and substitute brown rice where available to lower the glycemic load; the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre moderates postprandial glucose rise, but the high-GI white rice base and large serving size require active portion control and pairing with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried chicken karaage contributes saturated fat and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); beef contributes saturated fat and haem iron; combined fat 4.21 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat ~1.2 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request grilled chicken in place of karaage, and choose a smaller rice portion to reduce total saturated fat and caloric density; the dish's iron (2.55 mg/100 g) and potassium (327 mg/100 g) are beneficial, but the fried component and sodium load require moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef (arachidonic acid precursor) and deep-fried karaage (AGEs from high-temperature frying) are both pro-inflammatory; nightshade vegetables (potato) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or substitute the beef and karaage components; request grilled or steamed chicken and confirm the curry sauce does not contain nightshade-derived thickeners, as the combined pro-inflammatory load of this dish is high for active RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deluxe Curry Rice (デラックスカレーライス) is a Japanese-style composite rice dish served at Sukiya, a Japanese fast-casual chain operating in Singapore. The dish consists of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice topped with a rich, mildly spiced Japanese beef curry sauce, two pieces of crispy deep-fried chicken karaage, and one half-boiled egg. Japanese curry sauce is characteristically thicker, sweeter, and milder than South Asian curries, typically made from a roux block containing wheat flour, palm oil, curry spices, and umami seasonings. The karaage is marinated in soy sauce, ginger, and garlic before being coated in potato starch and deep-fried, yielding a crispy exterior and juicy interior. The half-boiled egg adds creaminess and additional protein. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium), making portion control important for glycaemic management. The dish is moderate in sodium and saturated fat, with the deep-fried karaage being the primary source of AGEs and additional fat. Clinically, it is suitable for most healthy adults in moderate portions but requires sodium and carbohydrate monitoring for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory combination of beef and deep-fried chicken.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Sukiya Deluxe Curry Rice?
Is Deluxe Curry Rice suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: All core per_100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Deluxe Curry Rice (cr_id G00010221), which carries a confidence of 0.95 and review_status of reviewed. Saturated fat is the only field estimated from a USDA SR Legacy analogy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style curry rice.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef curry with rice (closest approved-source analogy); HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB source despite the presence of egg and beef, which may reflect the composite dilution effect at the per-100g level — this value should be reviewed if a more granular breakdown becomes available. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese-style curry rice; no direct GI measurement for Sukiya Deluxe Curry Rice exists in the approved GI database. 5. Glycemic load per serving (29.3) is calculated for a 450 g serving: GL = GI × (carbohydrate_g/100 g × serving_g) / 100 = 65 × (25.19 × 4.5) / 100 = 65 × 1.1336 = 29.3. 6. The dish is classified as non-halal due to the presence of beef and deep-fried components whose halal certification status at Sukiya Singapore is not confirmed in approved sources.
